title: Generative Adversarial Nets
venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2014)
abstract: We propose a new framework for estimating generative models via an adversarial process, in which we simultaneously train two models: a generative model G that captures the data distribution, and a discriminative model D that estimates the probability that a sample came from the training data rather than G. The training procedure for G is to maximize the probability of D making a mistake. This framework corresponds to a minimax two-player game. In the space of arbitrary functions G and D, a unique solution exists, with G recovering the training data distribution and D equal to 1/2 everywhere. In the case where G and D are defined by multilayer perceptrons, the entire system can be trained with backpropagation.
role: Foundational GAN paper — establishes the minimax adversarial training framework used in our discriminator phases for adversarial latent regularisation (C3-C7).

title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
abstract: How can we perform efficient inference and learning in directed probabilistic models, in the presence of continuous latent variables with intractable posterior distributions, and large datasets? We introduce a stochastic variational inference and learning algorithm that scales to large datasets and, under some mild differentiability conditions, even works in the intractable case. We show that a reparameterization of the variational lower bound yields a lower bound estimator that can be straightforwardly optimized using standard stochastic gradient methods. For i.i.d. datasets with continuous latent variables per datapoint, posterior inference can be made especially efficient by fitting an approximate inference model (recognition model) to the intractable posterior using the proposed lower bound estimator.
role: VAE baseline (C2) — the reparameterisation trick and ELBO loss used directly in our C2 condition; also motivates probabilistic latent space structure that the adversarial prior in C3-C7 approximates.

title: Adversarial Autoencoders
abstract: In this paper, we propose the 'adversarial autoencoder' (AAE), which is a probabilistic autoencoder that uses the recently proposed generative adversarial networks (GAN) to perform variational inference by matching the aggregated posterior of the hidden code vector of the autoencoder with an arbitrary prior distribution. Matching the aggregated posterior to the prior ensures that generating from any part of prior space results in meaningful samples. As a result, the decoder of the adversarial autoencoder learns a deep generative model that maps the imposed prior to the data distribution. We show how the adversarial autoencoder can be used in applications such as semi-supervised classification, disentangling style and content of images, unsupervised clustering, dimensionality reduction and data visualization. We performed experiments on MNIST, Street View House Numbers and Toronto Face datasets and show that adversarial autoencoders achieve competitive results in generative modeling and semi-supervised classification tasks.
role: Direct architectural basis for C3-C7 — our CNN-AAE (C3) and all subsequent conditions extend AAE with RE-Attention and a second encoder. The three-phase training (reconstruction, discriminator, generator) is adapted from this work.

title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
venue: IEEE Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR 2016)
doi_note: Valid IEEE DOI — resolves to ieeexplore.ieee.org; doi.org HEAD returns HTTP 202 (IEEE auth redirect, not invalid)
abstract: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train. We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those used previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, instead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide comprehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8x deeper than VGG nets but still having lower complexity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error on the ImageNet test set. This result won the 1st place on the ILSVRC 2015 classification task.
role: Backbone for C5, C6, C7 — ResNet-18 (pretrained on ImageNet) replaces the scratch CNN encoder. The comparison C4 vs C5/C6/C7 tests whether pretrained residual features improve anomaly detection in chest X-rays.

title: Image Quality Assessment: From Error Visibility to Structural Similarity
venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing, vol. 13, no. 4, pp. 600–612
doi_note: Valid IEEE DOI — resolves to ieeexplore.ieee.org; doi.org HEAD returns HTTP 202 (IEEE auth redirect, not invalid)
abstract: Objective methods for assessing perceptual image quality traditionally attempted to quantify the visibility of errors (differences) between a distorted image and a reference image using a variety of known properties of the human visual system. Under this error sensitivity paradigm, the goal of image quality assessment is to accurately assess the visibility of the error signal and not the error itself. We propose a complementary framework for quality assessment based on the degradation of structural information. We consider three components of the image signal: luminance, contrast and structure. A structural similarity (SSIM) index is developed and its promise is demonstrated through intuitive examples, as well as comparison to both subjective ratings and state-of-the-art objective methods on a database of images compressed with JPEG and JPEG2000.
role: SSIM as anomaly signal — our RE-Attention mechanism uses (1 - SSIM) as the per-pixel reconstruction error map that guides the second encoding pass. SSIM captures perceptually meaningful structural degradation better than MSE for chest X-rays.

title: CBAM: Convolutional Block Attention Module
abstract: We propose Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), a simple yet effective attention module for feed-forward convolutional neural networks. Given an intermediate feature map, our module sequentially infers attention maps along two separate dimensions, channel and spatial, then the attention maps are multiplied to the input feature map for adaptive feature refinement. Because CBAM is a lightweight and general module, it can be integrated into any CNN architectures seamlessly with negligible overheads and is end-to-end trainable along with base CNNs. We validate our CBAM through extensive experiments on ImageNet-1K, MS COCO detection, and VOC 2007 detection datasets. Our experiments show consistent improvements in classification and detection performances with various models, demonstrating the wide applicability of CBAM.
role: Most closely related attention mechanism — CBAM applies sequential channel and spatial attention to feature maps. Our RE-Attention differs: it applies spatial attention to the input image guided by the reconstruction error map, not to intermediate feature maps. Key novelty distinction.

title: Adversarially Learned Anomaly Detection
venue: IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM 2018)
doi_note: Valid IEEE DOI — resolves to ieeexplore.ieee.org; doi.org HEAD returns HTTP 202 (IEEE auth redirect, not invalid)
abstract: Anomaly detection is a significant and hence well-studied problem. However, developing effective anomaly detection methods for complex and high-dimensional data remains a challenge. As Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are able to model the complex high-dimensional distributions of real-world data, they offer a promising approach to address this challenge. In this work, we propose an anomaly detection method, Adversarially Learned Anomaly Detection (ALAD) based on bi-directional GANs, that derives adversarially learned features for the anomaly detection task. ALAD then uses reconstruction errors based on these adversarially learned features to determine if a data sample is anomalous. ALAD builds on recent advances to ensure data-space and latent-space cycle-consistencies and stabilize GAN training, which results in significantly improved anomaly detection performance. ALAD achieves state-of-the-art performance on a range of image and tabular datasets while being several hundred-fold faster at test time than the only published GAN-based method.
role: Closest prior work using dual reconstruction + discriminator scoring for anomaly detection. Our method differs: ALAD uses bi-directional GANs in data space; we use a latent-space Gaussian prior + error-guided spatial attention applied to the image domain (chest X-ray).

title: Unsupervised Anomaly Detection with Generative Adversarial Networks to Guide Marker Discovery
venue: International Conference on Information Processing in Medical Imaging (IPMI 2017)
abstract: Obtaining models that capture imaging markers relevant for disease progression and treatment monitoring is challenging. Models are typically based on large amounts of data with annotated examples of known markers aiming at automating their detection. High annotation effort and the limitation to a closed set of known markers limit the power of such approaches. Here, we perform unsupervised learning to identify anomalies in imaging data as candidates for markers. We propose AnoGAN, a deep convolutional generative adversarial network to learn a manifold of normal anatomical variability, accompanied by a novel anomaly scoring scheme based on the mapping from image space to a latent space. Applied to new data, the model labels anomalies, and scores image patches indicating their fit into the learned distribution. Results on optical coherence tomography images of the retina demonstrate that the approach correctly identifies anomalous images and achieves segmentation performance on a par with human expert annotation of retinal fluid regions.
role: Pioneer of GAN-based image anomaly detection with latent space inversion scoring. Our approach avoids AnoGAN's slow inference-time optimisation by training a direct encoder (enc2) to map attended images to latent codes.

title: f-AnoGAN: Fast Unsupervised Anomaly Detection with Generative Adversarial Networks
abstract: Obtaining models that provide fast unsupervised anomaly detection and localization in high-dimensional data is challenging. Systematic screening for disease markers can massively accelerate biomarker discovery, and the subsequent development of precision diagnostics and personalized treatment strategies. We introduce f-AnoGAN, which extends the AnoGAN model with a fast inference pathway via a trained encoder. The encoder maps query images to the GAN's latent space, enabling fast anomaly scoring and localization. We demonstrate the approach on optical coherence tomography (OCT) images and show that it achieves improved performance on unsupervised anomaly detection compared to AnoGAN while being substantially faster.
role: Direct predecessor addressing AnoGAN's slow inference: adds a trained encoder for fast latent mapping. Our enc2 (second encoder) serves a similar function but with RE-Attention pre-processing to focus encoding on error-highlighted regions.

title: Context-Encoding Variational Autoencoder for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection
venue: Medical Image Computing and Computer-Assisted Intervention (MICCAI 2019)
abstract: Unsupervised learning can leverage large-scale data sources without the need for annotations. In this context, deep learning-based autoencoders have shown great potential in detecting anomalies in medical images. Autoencoder-based approaches typically aim to learn a compressed representation of normal data and thereby fail to reconstruct anomalous regions, enabling their detection via reconstruction errors. However, state-of-the-art anomaly scores that are based on reconstruction error alone suffer from low specificity. We address this shortcoming by combining reconstruction-based with density-based anomaly scoring. Experiments on the BraTS-2017 and ISLES-2015 segmentation benchmarks show that ceVAE achieves unsupervised ROC-AUCs of 0.95 and 0.89, respectively, substantially outperforming prior approaches.
role: Establishes the reconstruction + density dual-score paradigm for medical image anomaly detection. Our fusion score (SSIM + discriminator) follows this dual-signal principle but uses an adversarial prior instead of VAE density.

title: Autoencoders for Unsupervised Anomaly Segmentation in Brain MR Images: A Comparative Study
abstract: Autoencoders and their variants are commonly used for unsupervised anomaly detection in brain MRI. In absence of pathological data during training, anomalies can be detected at test time as outliers from the learned distribution of healthy samples. Many flavors of autoencoders have been proposed for this purpose: undercomplete autoencoders, variational autoencoders, adversarial autoencoders, context autoencoders and variational autoencoders with normalizing flows. In this comprehensive study, we review these models, assess their strengths and weaknesses, and compare them on three different datasets covering different pathologies: White Matter Hyperintensities, Glioblastoma and Autism Spectrum Disorder. We find that simple convolutional autoencoders with structural similarity loss (SSIM) outperform more complex models while being faster to train.
role: Benchmark showing simple AE + SSIM loss outperforms complex generative models for unsupervised anomaly detection in medical images — directly motivates our use of SSIM reconstruction error as the primary anomaly signal and attention guidance.

title: CheXNet: Radiologist-Level Pneumonia Detection on Chest X-Rays with Deep Learning
abstract: We develop an algorithm that can detect pneumonia from chest X-rays at a level exceeding practicing radiologists. Our algorithm, CheXNet, is a 121-layer convolutional neural network trained on ChestX-ray14, currently the largest publicly available chest X-ray dataset, containing over 100,000 frontal-view X-ray images with 14 diseases. Four practicing academic radiologists annotate a test set, on which we compare the performance of CheXNet to that of radiologists. We find that CheXNet exceeds average radiologist performance on the F1 metric. We extend CheXNet to detect all 14 diseases in ChestX-ray14 and achieve state of the art results on all 14 diseases.
role: Establishes deep CNNs (ResNet-121) for chest X-ray pathology detection. Motivates our use of pretrained ResNet features (C5-C7): if supervised ResNets detect pneumonia, do their features transfer to unsupervised anomaly detection?

title: Deep Learning for Anomaly Detection: A Review
venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 54, no. 2, article 38
doi_note: Valid ACM DOI — resolves to dl.acm.org; doi.org HEAD returns HTTP 403 (access restriction, not invalid DOI)
abstract: Anomaly detection, a.k.a. outlier detection or novelty detection, has been a lasting yet active research area in various research communities for several decades. There are still some unique problem complexities and challenges that require advanced approaches. In recent years, deep learning enabled anomaly detection, i.e., deep anomaly detection, has emerged as a critical direction. This paper surveys the research of deep anomaly detection with a comprehensive taxonomy, covering advancements in three high-level categories and 11 fine-grained categories of the methods. We review their key intuitions, objective functions, underlying assumptions, advantages and disadvantages, and discuss how they address the aforementioned challenges. We further discuss a set of possible future opportunities and new perspectives on addressing the challenges.
role: Primary survey reference for contextualising our method within the field — identifies reconstruction-based, generative, and one-class classification approaches; our RE-Attn-AAE bridges the first two.

title: Deep Learning for Anomaly Detection: A Survey
abstract: Anomaly detection is an important problem that has been well-studied within diverse research areas and application domains. The aim of this survey is two-fold, firstly we present a structured and comprehensive overview of research methods in deep learning-based anomaly detection. Furthermore, we review the adoption of these methods for anomaly across various application domains and assess their effectiveness. We categorize the methods by underlying assumptions, describe key differentiators between normal and anomalous patterns, and identify advantages, limitations, and computational complexity of each category. We also identify open research challenges and present possible future directions for deep anomaly detection.
role: Comprehensive taxonomy of deep anomaly detection — used to position our contribution across the deep one-class, reconstruction-based, and adversarial training paradigms.

title: Deep One-Class Classification
abstract: Despite the great advances made by deep learning in many machine learning problems, there is a relative dearth of deep learning approaches for anomaly detection. Those approaches which do exist involve networks trained to perform a task other than anomaly detection, namely generative models or compression, which are in turn adapted for use in anomaly detection. We present a new formalism for anomaly detection with deep neural networks that is amenable to convex optimization using standard neural network training methods. We introduce Deep SVDD, a method that trains a neural network while minimizing the volume of a hypersphere that encloses the network representations of the data, gaining the ability to extract the common factors of variation in the data. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on MNIST and CIFAR-10 image benchmark datasets as well as in the detection of adversarial examples.
role: Alternative deep one-class approach — trains encoder to compress normal data to a hypersphere. Contrasts with our adversarial prior: SVDD enforces compactness, we enforce Gaussianity. Both structure the latent space for anomaly detection.

title: ARAE: An Adaptive Robust AutoEncoder for Network Anomaly Detection
venue: Journal of Cyber Security, vol. 7, no. 1
doi_note: Valid DOI — verified resolves to techscience.com/JCS
abstract: This paper introduces ARAE, a framework that dynamically balances reconstruction fidelity with latent space regularization through learnable loss weighting. The model employs multi-head attention to capture feature dependencies and combines multiple anomaly indicators into an adaptive scoring mechanism. Testing across four benchmark datasets demonstrates ARAE substantially outperforms existing autoencoder variants and classical methods, with ablation studies validating the importance of adaptive components. The framework provides a robust, self-tuning solution for modern network intrusion detection systems without requiring manual hyperparameter optimization.
role: Contemporary autoencoder + attention method for network anomaly detection. Shares multi-head attention + adaptive scoring with our approach; key difference is our use of SSIM reconstruction error as explicit attention guidance (RE-Attention) vs feature-level attention in ARAE.

title: Improving Performance of Autoencoder-Based Network Anomaly Detection on NSL-KDD Dataset
doi_note: Verified: doi.org/10.1109/ACCESS.2021.3116612 → HTTP 302 → ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9552882/ (corrected from erroneous DOI in manual_lookup.txt)
abstract: Network anomaly detection using autoencoder-based methods is a well-studied but challenging problem. This paper proposes a novel 5-layer autoencoder architecture better suited for network anomaly detection tasks, evaluated on the NSL-KDD dataset—a standard benchmark for intrusion detection. The study investigates performance improvements including architectural depth, regularisation strategies, and threshold optimisation to enhance detection accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed model achieves improved detection rates over prior autoencoder variants on the NSL-KDD benchmark.
role: Autoencoder baseline on NSL-KDD/network anomaly detection — useful as contrast: our method targets image-domain anomalies (chest X-ray) with spatial attention, while this work targets tabular network traffic with architecture depth and regularisation as primary levers.
